refinementHistory.title: Work History Index
refinementHistory.subtitle: The Qdrant vector store that grounds refinement's SP suggestions. Completed, SP-assigned work items are indexed here; for a new item, the LLM references the 3–5 closest matches.

refinementHistory.empty.hint: Go to Refinement → click Backfill History.
refinementHistory.items.title: Items
refinementHistory.items.footer: Total {total} results · {pageSize} per page · Filters applied server-side
refinementHistory.col.title: Title
refinement.backfill.needAzureProject: Please select an Azure project first.
refinement.backfill.needAzureTeam: Select a team first — otherwise the query pulls every team's work items and hits Azure's 20k limit.

refinement.backfill.needAzureTeamShort: Select a team above (project-wide hits the 20k cap)
refinement.backfill.tooltipAzure: Indexes completed Azure work items for the selected project+team into Qdrant
refinement.backfill.tooltipJira: Indexes completed Jira issues for the selected project into Qdrant
refinement.backfill.statusCapped: hit cap, there may be more
refinement.indexLink.title: Index has {count} completed items
refinement.indexLink.subtitle: SP distribution, top contributors, sample items — see details
refinement.runModal.subtitle: Pick a model and language. Similar past items from your index are auto-injected.
refinement.runModal.hint: The SP suggestion is grounded on your team's prior SP distribution.
skills.title: Team Skill Catalog
skills.subtitle: Reusable patterns extracted from completed tasks. Relevant skills are auto-injected into agent prompts — team solutions compound instead of getting rediscovered.
skills.empty.title: No skills yet
skills.empty.hint: They're auto-extracted as tasks complete. You can also add them manually.
skills.field.name: Name (short imperative — e.g. Add noindex to 404 pages)
skills.field.promptFragment: Prompt fragment — the agent sees this verbatim
runtimes.title: Compute Environments
runtimes.subtitle: Machines that execute agent tasks. The local CLI bridge auto-registers (claude/codex on your PATH); cloud daemons are added manually. Each runtime reports which CLIs it has available and its last heartbeat.
runtimes.empty.title: No runtimes registered
runtimes.empty.hint: To enroll your host bridge, set AGENA_JWT + AGENA_TENANT_SLUG in your shell and restart bridge-server.mjs. Or add a cloud daemon manually via "New Runtime".
runtimes.field.name: Name (e.g. ali-mac, prod-runtime-01)
landing.refinementTitle: Story Points grounded on your team's history
landing.refinementSubtitle: The LLM pulls your team's closed work items (final SP + assignee + PR titles + branches) from a vector index and anchors every new estimate on the closest matches — by name. No generic estimates, your velocity and your patterns.
landing.refinementStep1Desc: One click pulls every closed, SP-assigned item from Azure DevOps / Jira into Qdrant. Each is embedded with title + description + branch names + PR titles.
landing.refinementStep2Desc: The new item is queried as a vector; hits below cosine 0.55 are dropped. Top-5 survivors are returned, with same-type items (Bug/Story/Task) preferred when possible.
landing.refinementStep3Desc: Each hit goes into the LLM prompt with its SP badge, similarity %, previous assignee and linked PR titles. The model reasons "closest is #X, Ali did it at 3 SP."
landing.refinementStep4Desc: SP badge + confidence + rationale naming the prior items it's based on + a paste-ready comment. One click writes it back to Azure / Jira.
landing.refinementExampleQuote: Example: "#62840 (Ali, 5 SP) and #61206 (Ayşe, 3 SP) are closest. Auth cache invalidation, closer to #62840 — suggesting 5 SP."
landing.skillsSubtitle: Every completed task turns into a reusable skill: approach, prompt fragment, touched files, tags — all in a vector index. When a new task arrives, the closest skills are prepended to the agent's system prompt. Your team never discovers the same solution twice.
landing.skillsStep1Desc: Agent writes code, opens the PR, task marks completed. A fire-and-forget job kicks the Skill Librarian.
landing.skillsStep2Desc: OpenAI / Claude CLI / Codex CLI summarises title + description + reviewed code into { name, approach_summary, prompt_fragment, tags }. Skipped when confidence < 50 so too-unique tasks don't pollute the catalog.
landing.skillsStep3Desc: Upsert with kind='skill' payload, org-scoped, distinct from refinement history. A DB row is created too, powering the catalog UI with usage counters.
landing.skillsStep4Desc: Before the agent runs, top-3 skills above the relevance threshold are retrieved and prepended to the system prompt as a RELEVANT TEAM SKILLS block. Works in Claude CLI, Codex CLI, classic pipeline — all four modes.
landing.skillsExampleQuote: Result: after ~50 tasks, the catalog carries 20+ team-specific patterns ("Use ArrayHelper::safeGet on our webservice", "Order endpoints go through the OrderContext factory"). New devs or agents learn your codebase's conventions in minutes, not weeks.
landing.runtimesSubtitle: Your laptop, your teammate's machine, a cloud daemon — each registers as a Runtime. Token-based enrollment, 30s heartbeat, live status. Assign a task to a specific runtime (routing ships next).
landing.runtimesStep1Desc: bridge-server.mjs reads AGENA_JWT + AGENA_TENANT_SLUG. Absent → prior behaviour. Present → proceeds to enrollment.
landing.runtimesStep2Desc: POST /runtimes/register with name, platform, discovered CLIs (claude/codex). Backend mints a one-shot token, stores only a sha-256 hash, bridge persists to ~/.agena/runtime.json.
landing.runtimesStep3Desc: Heartbeat every 30s with X-Runtime-Token, refreshing CLI list + daemon version. UI polls every 10s; status dot (green/grey/red) is live.
landing.runtimesStep4Desc: GET /runtimes/{id}/tasks/next is already stubbed. Daemons will poll it for work; you'll be able to pin a task to a specific runtime from the UI.
landing.runtimesExampleQuote: Example: three teammates, each running the bridge on their mac. Three green runtimes in the dashboard. Fire a heavy test at Ali's machine while yours is busy — no build-env contention.
landing.nudgeSubtitle: Every blocked Azure / Jira ticket in the sprint gets a Ping button. Agena reads the thread, checks the last reply, and if it's been silent for 24h, an LLM drafts a short polite status-check — in the target language, signed by Agena — and posts it back with an @mention. 48h cooldown + DB-persisted history, so nobody ever gets pinged twice.
landing.nudgeStep1Desc: Fetches the comment thread from Azure DevOps or Jira and measures hours-since-last-activity. Anything <24h old is skipped — no noise.
landing.nudgeStep2Desc: Pick the LLM at send time: Claude CLI + sonnet (via the host bridge), Codex CLI, OpenAI, Gemini, or HAL. Claude failures auto-fall-back to Codex on the same bridge.
landing.nudgeStep3Desc: Message is generated in the chosen language (7 locales), wrapped with <at>@Display Name</at> so Azure renders a real mention, and signed "written by Agena via {model}" for full transparency.
landing.nudgeStep4Desc: Every successful nudge is stored in nudge_history. The UI badges already-pinged items with "Agena pinged Xh ago" and a 48h cooldown blocks accidental spam.
landing.nudgeExampleQuote: @Ali No progress for 45 hours on the new node type for Agena Flow Editor — can you share a quick status? — 🤖 Agena via claude_cli · sonnet
landing.cliTitle: Install, log in, and run the daemon — one command each
landing.cliSubtitle: brew install aozyildirim/tap/agena · agena setup · device-code OAuth + auto-enrollment. No JWT copy-paste, keychain-backed auth.
landing.cliSectionSubtitle: Install once (brew or npm), log in with device-code OAuth, and get every dashboard workflow — tasks, skills, sprint refinement, runtimes — as a single-binary command. JWT held in the OS keychain; no copy-paste.
landing.cliStep1Desc: agena login opens a browser for RFC 8628 device-code, stores the JWT in macOS Keychain / libsecret / Windows Credential Manager. Switch orgs with agena org switch <slug>.
landing.cliStep2Desc: agena task create -t "Fix login" --assign enqueues for the AI agent; task list / show / logs give you the full trail without opening the dashboard.
landing.cliStep3Desc: agena skill search "nullable pointer" vector-matches the team's auto-extracted patterns; list / show / delete round out the catalog.
landing.cliStep4Desc: agena refinement backfill indexes your Azure/Jira history into Qdrant. agena refinement analyze gives grounded SP estimates before the planning call.
landing.cliExampleQuote: Install: brew install aozyildirim/tap/agena · or npm install -g @agenaai/cli. The bridge is bundled — agena daemon start just works.

landing.integrationsSubtitle: All integrations are unified in one panel and events flow end-to-end automatically.
landing.newrelicTitle: New Relic + Sentry Errors → AI-Fixed PRs
landing.newrelicSubtitle: Auto-detect production issues from New Relic APM and Sentry, create fix tasks, and let AI agents resolve them with pull requests — hands-free.
landing.newrelicF1Desc: Connect your New Relic account and automatically import error groups from APM entities. Deduplication prevents duplicate tasks.
landing.newrelicF2Desc: Extracts file paths, line numbers, and affected endpoints from error messages. Agents know exactly where to fix.
landing.newrelicF3Desc: Map New Relic entities to your repositories. When errors are imported, the system knows which codebase to target.
landing.newrelicF4Desc: Enable auto-import per entity. The worker polls New Relic every 5 minutes and creates tasks for new errors automatically.
landing.newrelicFlowDesc: Use the New Relic node in visual flows to fetch errors, filter by conditions, and trigger AI agents to generate fixes.
landing.sentryFlowDesc: Stream Sentry issues into your fix pipeline, apply triage conditions, and let AI agents generate patch PRs automatically.
landing.chatopsTitle: Real-time delivery visibility with Slack and Teams
landing.chatopsSubtitle: With Notification Router, pipeline events, PR outcomes, failure signals, and approval steps are automatically routed to the right channel.
landing.slackDesc: Task state transitions, PR creation events, and failed run alerts are sent instantly to the selected channel.
landing.teamsDesc: For enterprise teams, pending approvals, cost/limit warnings, and result summaries are published into Teams feed.
landing.liveTaskBoardDesc: Track queued, running, and completed tasks with per-task AI timeline and cost breakdown.
landing.aiAssignmentDesc: One click "Assign to AI" with Azure/Jira import actions inside the dashboard.
landing.widgetsTitle: Watch flow and agent engine in real time
landing.widgetsSubtitle: Track flow orchestration and role-based agent behavior in a single view.
landing.flowShowcaseWords: See The Real Flow Design Clearly
landing.flowShowcaseDesc: This visual shows how task stages move through the agent pipeline. Each step runs with live logs and control layers.
landing.flowShowcasePoint1: Stage transitions are observable and auditable.
landing.flowShowcasePoint2: Approval gates and risk signals are visible on the flow.
landing.flowShowcasePoint3: Code generation and PR lifecycle are unified in one schema.
landing.patronTitle: Control the whole flow from one cockpit
landing.patronDesc: Patron Mode combines workload, risks, and AI spend in one panel. Make fast decisions and steer the team instantly.
landing.patronLine3: Recommendation: assign +1 agent to QA queue
landing.vectorSubtitle: During flow execution, the system retrieves similar tasks from Qdrant vector DB to strengthen prompt context. This drives more consistent, faster, and more accurate code generation inside the same repo scope.
landing.vectorHowDesc: During fetch_context, similar records are retrieved and injected into analyze/generate/review stages.
landing.vectorStep1: Task title + description is transformed into an embedding query.
landing.vectorStep2: Tenant-scoped similar records are searched with organization_id filter.
landing.vectorStep3: Top matches are summarized into context summary.
landing.vectorStep4: After finalize, new output is written back as memory.
landing.vectorFieldsDesc: Primary fields stored in Qdrant payload:
landing.vectorFieldOrg: Used as tenant isolation filter during retrieval.
landing.vectorFieldInput: Snapshot of task title + effective description.
landing.vectorFieldOutput: Snapshot of finalized generated code output.
landing.vectorEmbedDesc: Content types currently matched through memory:
landing.vectorEmbed2: Task title + effective prompt context (write side)
landing.vectorEmbed4: Context summary enriched by repo/tenant rules
landing.capabilitiesSubtitle: Make AI delivery safer, governed, and predictable with dependency graph, PR risk scoring, and tenant playbook controls.
landing.newSubtitle: Live streaming, safer code generation, and clearer task visibility are now showcased with animated cards on the landing page.
landing.newItem1Desc: Task detail now streams logs in real-time via SSE; follow stage updates without refreshing.
landing.newItem2Desc: Agents now stay aligned with repository stack; absolute-path and stack-drift outputs are filtered at backend level.
landing.newItem3Desc: Execution progress is computed with stage aliases, reducing false pending states in the UI.
landing.newItem4Desc: Task duration, token, cost, and run telemetry are now easier to track in the interface.
landing.capDependencyDesc: Connect tasks, surface blockers, and automatically prevent assignment before prerequisite work is completed.
landing.capRiskDesc: Generate a live risk score per task using file churn, critical-path touches, and diff scale.
landing.capPlaybookDesc: Each organization defines coding rules and the agent pipeline automatically injects them into execution prompts.
landing.capStoryDesc: Agents interpret intent more accurately with business context, acceptance criteria, and edge-case notes.
landing.capGuardrailDesc: Prevent budget overrun with per-task token/cost limits, then auto-stop or request approval at threshold.
landing.flowWidgetDesc: See node-graph stages, approval gates, and execution chain in real time.
landing.agentWidgetDesc: Monitor model, specialization level, and pipeline contribution for each role with a live distribution.
landing.heroDesc: AGENA is an agentic AI platform powered by pixel agent technology. It runs an autonomous PM → Dev → Reviewer pipeline that writes production code, reviews quality, and opens GitHub PRs with full task telemetry.
landing.featuresTitle: Everything your team needs to ship faster
landing.feature1Desc: JWT auth, org-level isolation, usage limits, and billing controls baked in from day one.
landing.feature2Desc: Pixel agent architecture with LangGraph state machine providing full observability across each AI stage of your pipeline.
landing.feature3Desc: Branch, commit, and PR generation with traceable task links and review summaries.
landing.feature4Desc: Prompt caching, model routing, and token/cost tracking per organization.
landing.step1Desc: Pull work from Jira/Azure or create directly in the dashboard. AI parses intent and context.
landing.step2Desc: PM, Dev, and Reviewer agents collaborate through LangGraph states with full telemetry.
landing.step3Desc: Reviewed output becomes a GitHub PR with timeline, logs, and quality scores.
landing.ctaDesc: Create your workspace and run your first autonomous delivery cycle today.
landing.faq1A: AGENA is an agentic AI platform that autonomously generates code, reviews quality, and creates pull requests. It runs a PM → Developer → Reviewer → Finalizer pipeline powered by pixel agent technology, turning your backlog into production-ready PRs in minutes.
landing.faq2A: Agentic AI refers to AI systems that can autonomously plan, execute, and adapt to achieve complex goals. Unlike simple AI assistants, agentic AI agents work independently through multi-step workflows — analyzing tasks, generating code, reviewing quality, and shipping features without constant human guidance.
landing.faq3A: Pixel agent technology is AGENA's visual orchestration layer that represents each AI agent as an interactive pixel character. It provides real-time visibility into your autonomous AI workforce — showing which agents are active, what they're working on, and the progress of each task through the pipeline.
landing.faq4Q: Which platforms does AGENA integrate with?
landing.faq4A: AGENA integrates with GitHub and Azure DevOps for PR automation, Jira for task import, Slack and Microsoft Teams for ChatOps notifications, and supports OpenAI and Google Gemini as LLM providers.
landing.faq5A: Yes! AGENA offers a free tier with 5 tasks per month, token usage tracking, and community support. The Pro plan ($49/month) includes unlimited tasks, priority worker throughput, team invites, and billing via Stripe or Iyzico.
landing.faq6A: Yes, AGENA is fully open source under the MIT license. You can self-host it or use the managed platform. The source code is available on GitHub at github.com/aozyildirim/Agena.

contact.faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
contact.faq1Q: How do I get started with AGENA?
contact.faq1A: Sign up for a free account, connect your GitHub or Azure DevOps repository, and create your first task. AGENA's AI agents will analyze, generate code, and open a pull request automatically.
contact.faq2A: Yes! AGENA is fully open source under the MIT license. You can self-host it or use the managed platform. The source code is available on GitHub.
contact.faq3A: AGENA integrates with GitHub, Azure DevOps, Jira, Slack, and Microsoft Teams. We support OpenAI and Google Gemini as LLM providers.
contact.faq4Q: Can I use my own LLM API keys?
contact.faq4A: Absolutely. AGENA supports bring-your-own-key for OpenAI and Google Gemini. Configure your API keys in the dashboard settings.
contact.faq5Q: How does the free tier work?
contact.faq5A: The free tier includes 5 AI tasks per month with full feature access. No credit card required. Upgrade to Pro for unlimited tasks and priority processing.
contact.faq6A: AGENA never stores your source code. All repository access is scoped via OAuth tokens and code is processed in isolated sessions. Self-hosting gives you full control.

useCases.title: What Can You Build with Agentic AI?
useCases.subtitle: AGENA's pixel agent technology powers autonomous development workflows for teams of all sizes.
useCases.ctaTitle: Ready to automate your development workflow?
useCases.ctaDesc: Start free and let AGENA's agentic AI pipeline handle the rest.
useCases.uc1.title: Autonomous Code Generation
useCases.uc1.subtitle: From task description to production code
useCases.uc1.desc: AGENA's agentic AI pipeline takes a task from your backlog and autonomously generates production-grade code. The developer agent analyzes your codebase, follows existing patterns, and produces clean, reviewable code.
useCases.uc1.b1: Reduce time from task to PR by 10x
useCases.uc1.b2: Consistent code quality across all generated PRs
useCases.uc1.b4: Full context awareness via vector memory
useCases.uc2.title: AI-Powered Pull Request Automation
useCases.uc2.subtitle: Branch, commit, and PR creation on autopilot
useCases.uc2.desc: Stop manually creating branches and writing PR descriptions. AGENA's finalizer agent automatically creates feature branches, makes clean commits, and opens pull requests with full task context on GitHub or Azure DevOps.
useCases.uc2.b1: Zero manual Git operations for AI-generated code
useCases.uc2.b2: PR descriptions include full task context and changes
useCases.uc2.b3: Automatic branch naming following your conventions
useCases.uc3.title: Sprint Acceleration
useCases.uc3.subtitle: Complete more tasks per sprint with AI agents
useCases.uc3.desc: Augment your development team with agentic AI. AGENA handles routine development tasks autonomously while your engineers focus on complex architecture and business logic.
useCases.uc3.b1: AI handles routine tasks, humans handle complexity
useCases.uc3.b3: Pixel agent dashboard shows AI workforce status
useCases.uc4.title: Automated Code Review
useCases.uc4.subtitle: AI reviewer agent catches issues before humans
useCases.uc4.desc: AGENA's reviewer agent independently reviews all generated code for bugs, security vulnerabilities, and best practices before creating the PR.
useCases.uc4.b4: Reduced review burden on senior engineers
useCases.uc5.title: Context-Aware Development with Vector Memory
useCases.uc5.subtitle: AI agents learn from your past tasks
useCases.uc5.desc: AGENA's Qdrant-powered vector memory stores context from previously completed tasks. When a new task arrives, AI agents automatically retrieve similar past solutions, enabling smarter code generation.
useCases.uc5.b1: AI improves with every completed task
useCases.uc5.b2: Similar past solutions inform new code generation
useCases.uc6.title: ChatOps Integration
useCases.uc6.subtitle: Manage AI agents from Slack and Teams
useCases.uc6.desc: Trigger AI agent tasks, receive real-time progress updates, and review generated PRs directly from Slack or Microsoft Teams.
useCases.uc6.b1: Trigger tasks from Slack or Teams
useCases.uc6.b3: Review AI-generated PRs without leaving chat
useCases.uc6.b4: Team-wide visibility into AI agent activity
useCases.uc7.title: Multi-Repo Orchestration
useCases.uc7.subtitle: One task, multiple repositories, parallel PRs
useCases.uc7.desc: Assign a single task to multiple repositories simultaneously. AGENA runs independent AI agent pipelines for each repo, generating separate branches and pull requests in parallel. Ideal for cross-cutting changes that span backend, frontend, and shared libraries.
useCases.uc7.b1: One task creates PRs across multiple repos
useCases.uc7.b2: Each repo gets its own full agent pipeline
useCases.uc7.b4: Per-repo progress tracking on the dashboard

glossary.label: Glossary
glossary.title: AGENA Technical Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Key concepts and terms in AI-powered software development
glossary.term1.short: AI systems that autonomously plan, execute, and iterate on tasks
glossary.term1.full: Agentic AI refers to artificial intelligence systems capable of autonomous decision-making, planning, and execution. Unlike traditional AI that responds to single prompts, agentic AI maintains context, breaks down complex tasks into steps, and iteratively refines its output. In AGENA, agentic AI powers a pipeline of specialized agents (PM, Developer, Reviewer, Finalizer) that collaborate to generate production-ready code.
glossary.term2.short: AGENA's visual AI character representing agent roles in the office view
glossary.term2.full: Pixel Agents are animated visual characters displayed in AGENA's Boss Mode office view. Each pixel agent represents a specific AI role — PM, Developer, Reviewer, or Finalizer — and shows real-time activity as it works on tasks. They provide an intuitive, gamified interface for monitoring multi-agent collaboration.
glossary.term3.short: Foundation models like GPT-5 and Gemini that power AI agents
glossary.term3.full: Large Language Models are deep neural networks trained on vast text corpora to understand and generate human language. AGENA uses LLMs such as GPT-5 and Gemini as the reasoning engine behind its AI agents. Each agent can be configured with a different LLM provider and model, allowing organizations to balance cost, speed, and quality.
glossary.term4.short: Enhancing LLM output with retrieved context from codebases
glossary.term4.full: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines LLM generation with information retrieval from external sources. In AGENA, RAG pulls relevant code snippets, documentation, and past task outputs from vector memory (Qdrant) to provide the LLM with precise context. This reduces hallucinations and ensures generated code aligns with the existing codebase.
glossary.term5.short: Multiple AI agents collaborating as PM, Developer, Reviewer, and Finalizer
glossary.term5.full: A Multi-Agent System uses several specialized AI agents that work together on a shared task. AGENA's pipeline includes a PM agent (requirements analysis), Developer agent (code generation), Reviewer agent (quality assurance), and Finalizer agent (PR preparation). Each agent has distinct system prompts, goals, and tool access, enabling division of labor and higher output quality.
glossary.term6.short: Graph-based orchestration framework for AI agent pipelines
glossary.term6.full: LangGraph is a framework for building stateful, multi-step AI applications using directed graphs. AGENA uses LangGraph to define a 5-node pipeline: fetch_context, analyze, generate_code, review_code, and finalize. Each node can invoke different LLM models and tools, with state passed between nodes for context continuity.
glossary.term7.short: Framework for defining AI agent roles, goals, and collaboration patterns
glossary.term7.full: CrewAI is a framework for orchestrating role-based AI agents. In AGENA, CrewAI defines agent personas (PM, Developer, Reviewer, Finalizer) with specific roles, goals, backstories, and tool access. Agents collaborate through a structured workflow, passing artifacts between stages to produce reviewed, production-quality code.
glossary.term8.short: Embedding-based storage (Qdrant) for semantic code search and context
glossary.term8.full: Vector Memory stores text as high-dimensional embeddings, enabling semantic similarity search. AGENA uses Qdrant as its vector database to index code snippets, documentation, and past task outputs. When an agent needs context, it queries vector memory to find the most relevant information, improving code generation accuracy and consistency.
glossary.term9.short: DevOps Research and Assessment: deployment frequency, lead time, CFR, MTTR
glossary.term9.full: DORA Metrics are four key indicators of software delivery performance: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate (CFR), and Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR). AGENA tracks these metrics automatically, giving engineering teams visibility into how AI-assisted development impacts their delivery velocity and reliability.
glossary.term10.short: Visual n8n-style automation builder for custom AI workflows
glossary.term10.full: The Flow Builder is AGENA's visual drag-and-drop interface for creating custom AI automation workflows. Inspired by n8n, it supports node types including Agent, Azure DevOps, GitHub, HTTP, Condition, Notify, and Trigger. Nodes communicate via context variables, allowing complex branching logic and multi-step automations without writing code.
glossary.term11.short: Crafting system prompts to control AI agent behavior and output quality
glossary.term11.full: Prompt Engineering is the practice of designing and refining system prompts that guide LLM behavior. AGENA stores prompts in a database and provides Prompt Studio, a UI for editing prompts at runtime. This allows teams to fine-tune agent behavior, output format, and quality standards without redeploying code.
glossary.term12.short: AI agent that reviews generated code for bugs, security, and best practices
glossary.term12.full: The Code Review Agent is a specialized AI agent in AGENA's pipeline that analyzes generated code for correctness, security vulnerabilities, performance issues, and adherence to coding standards. It produces structured feedback that the Developer agent can use to iterate, ensuring the final output meets production quality standards.
glossary.term13.short: Automatic branch creation, code commit, and pull request generation
glossary.term13.full: PR Automation is the end-to-end process of creating a Git branch, committing AI-generated code, and opening a pull request — all without manual intervention. AGENA supports both GitHub and Azure DevOps, automatically assigning reviewers, adding labels, and linking work items. This eliminates the manual overhead of preparing code for review.
glossary.term14.short: Controlling AI agents via chat commands in Slack, Teams, or Telegram
glossary.term14.full: ChatOps brings AI agent control directly into team communication platforms. With AGENA's ChatOps integration, developers can trigger tasks, check agent status, and receive PR notifications in Slack, Microsoft Teams, or Telegram. This streamlines workflows by keeping AI interactions in the same place where teams already collaborate.
glossary.term15.short: Architecture where each organization has isolated data and configurations
glossary.term15.full: Multi-Tenant architecture allows a single application instance to serve multiple organizations with strict data isolation. In AGENA, every database query is scoped by organization_id, ensuring that one tenant's code, prompts, configurations, and usage data are never accessible to another. Each organization can customize agents, flows, and integrations independently.
glossary.term16.short: AI-powered analysis of backlog items with story point suggestions
glossary.term16.full: Sprint Refinement in AGENA uses AI to analyze backlog items from Jira or Azure DevOps, suggesting story points, identifying dependencies, and flagging ambiguous requirements. This accelerates the refinement ceremony by providing data-driven estimates and structured breakdowns before the team discussion begins.
glossary.term17.short: Organization-wide coding rules and conventions that guide AI code generation
glossary.term17.full: A Playbook is a set of organization-specific coding standards, architectural patterns, and conventions that AGENA's AI agents follow when generating code. Playbooks ensure consistency across AI-generated output by encoding rules like naming conventions, error handling patterns, testing requirements, and framework-specific best practices.
glossary.term18.short: Per-task limit on LLM tokens to control cost and output length
glossary.term18.full: A Token Budget sets the maximum number of LLM tokens (input + output) that can be consumed by a single task. AGENA allows organizations to configure token budgets per agent, per task type, or globally, providing cost control and preventing runaway LLM usage. Usage dashboards show real-time token consumption against budgets.
glossary.term19.short: Zero-downtime deployment strategy using two identical environments
glossary.term19.full: Blue-Green Deployment is a release strategy that maintains two identical production environments (blue and green). At any time, one serves live traffic while the other is updated. AGENA uses this pattern for its frontend, rebuilding one environment at a time behind an Nginx load balancer to ensure zero downtime during deployments.
glossary.term20.short: HTTP callbacks for real-time event notifications between systems
glossary.term20.full: Webhooks are HTTP POST requests sent automatically when specific events occur. In AGENA, webhooks notify external systems when tasks complete, PRs are created, or agent status changes. They enable real-time integration with CI/CD pipelines, monitoring tools, and custom automation without polling the API.
glossary.ctaTitle: Ready to put these concepts into action?
glossary.ctaDesc: Start using AGENA's AI agents to automate your development workflow.

landing.mr2: Parallel execution — each repo runs its own AI agents simultaneously
landing.mr3: Per-repo locking prevents conflicts between concurrent operations
landing.mr4: Unified dashboard shows all PRs and their status in one view
landing.depsDesc: Define execution order between tasks. When a dependency completes, the next task in the chain starts automatically — no manual intervention needed.
landing.dep1: Set dependencies at task creation or anytime after
landing.dep2: Auto-queue: dependent tasks start when blockers complete
landing.dep3: Cycle detection prevents circular dependency chains
landing.dep4: Visual dependency flow shows the full execution pipeline
useCases.uc8.title: Task Dependencies
useCases.uc8.subtitle: Chain tasks with automatic execution order
useCases.uc8.desc: Define which tasks must complete before others can start. The worker checks dependencies before executing — blocked tasks wait automatically. When a blocker completes, dependent tasks auto-queue and start without manual intervention.
useCases.uc8.b1: Set dependencies at creation time or update them later
useCases.uc8.b2: Auto-queue: dependent tasks start when all blockers complete
useCases.uc8.b3: Cycle detection prevents circular dependency chains
useCases.uc8.b4: Visual dependency flow shows the full execution pipeline
promptStudio.refinementSystemSummary: System instructions for the sprint refinement analyst
promptStudio.refinementDescriptionSummar: Work item template with variables ({title}, {description}, etc.)
promptStudio.refinementExpectedSummary: JSON output format specification for refinement results
vs.title: Compare AGENA
vs.subtitle: See how AGENA compares to other AI coding tools
vs.indexHero: Find the right AI development tool for your team
vs.ctaSubtitle: Start free and see the difference an agentic AI pipeline makes.
vs.cursor.title: AGENA vs Cursor
vs.cursor.subtitle: Multi-agent pipeline vs AI-powered IDE
vs.cursor.hook: Cursor excels at inline editing. AGENA automates the entire workflow — from task to PR.
vs.cursor.approach.competitor: AI-powered IDE with inline code completion
vs.cursor.why1: End-to-end automation: AGENA takes tasks from backlog to merged PR without manual steps.
vs.cursor.why2: Multi-agent review: Built-in reviewer agent catches bugs before the PR is even opened.
vs.cursor.why3: Team orchestration: Parallel multi-repo execution for large codebases.
vs.cursor.why4: Self-hosted option: Run on your own infrastructure with Docker Compose for full control.
vs.cursor.metaTitle: AGENA vs Cursor – AI Code Generation Alternative Comparison
vs.cursor.metaDesc: Compare AGENA and Cursor for AI code generation. See how AGENA's multi-agent pipeline and autonomous PR creation stack up against Cursor's AI IDE.
vs.copilot.title: AGENA vs GitHub Copilot
vs.copilot.subtitle: Autonomous AI pipeline vs code completion assistant
vs.copilot.hook: Copilot suggests code line by line. AGENA ships entire features autonomously — from task to PR.
vs.copilot.approach.competitor: Code completion + chat assistant in IDE
vs.copilot.why1: Full workflow automation: AGENA handles the entire dev cycle, not just code suggestions.
vs.copilot.why2: No context switching: Tasks go from Jira/Azure DevOps directly to PRs without leaving AGENA.
vs.copilot.why3: Autonomous review: The built-in reviewer agent catches issues before humans even see the code.
vs.copilot.why4: Open source and self-hosted: Complete control over your AI-powered development pipeline.
vs.copilot.metaTitle: AGENA vs GitHub Copilot – AI Pull Request Automation Alternative
vs.copilot.metaDesc: Compare AGENA and GitHub Copilot. See why teams choose AGENA's autonomous AI pipeline over Copilot's code completion for end-to-end PR automation.
vs.devin.title: AGENA vs Devin
vs.devin.subtitle: Open-source multi-agent platform vs proprietary AI developer
vs.devin.hook: Devin works on one task at a time. AGENA orchestrates multiple agents in parallel across repos.
vs.devin.why1: 10x more affordable: AGENA Pro at $49/mo vs Devin's $500/mo per seat.
vs.devin.why2: Parallel execution: Run multiple tasks across repos simultaneously, not one at a time.
vs.devin.why3: Sprint integration: Auto-sync with Jira and Azure DevOps for seamless project management.
vs.devin.why4: Open source: Full transparency, self-hosted deployment, and community-driven development.
vs.devin.metaTitle: AGENA vs Devin – AI Developer Agent Alternative Comparison
vs.devin.metaDesc: Compare AGENA and Devin AI developer. See how AGENA offers multi-agent orchestration, sprint management, and self-hosted deployment at a fraction of the cost.
vs.codex.title: AGENA vs OpenAI Codex
vs.codex.subtitle: Full-stack AI platform vs CLI coding agent
vs.codex.hook: Codex is great for single tasks via CLI. AGENA provides a full platform with dashboard, team features, and automated PR workflows.
vs.codex.why1: Full platform experience: Dashboard, analytics, team management — not just a CLI tool.
vs.codex.why2: Automated PR pipeline: From task description to reviewed pull request, fully automated.
vs.codex.why3: Team collaboration: RBAC, sprint management, and ChatOps for the whole team.
vs.codex.why4: Multi-provider LLM: Use OpenAI, Google Gemini, or other providers — no vendor lock-in.
vs.codex.metaTitle: AGENA vs OpenAI Codex – AI Code Generation Platform Alternative
vs.codex.metaDesc: Compare AGENA and OpenAI Codex CLI. See how AGENA's full-stack AI platform with dashboard, team features, and PR automation goes beyond CLI-based coding.
vs.multica.title: AGENA vs Multica
vs.multica.subtitle: Agentic coding platforms, head to head
vs.multica.hook: Multica treats agents as team members on a board. AGENA goes a step further — agents ship across multiple repos in parallel, with sprint-aware estimation, DORA metrics, and visual flow orchestration baked in.
vs.multica.approach.agena: Multi-agent LangGraph pipeline (PM → Planner → Dev → Review → Finalize) with CrewAI role agents
vs.multica.approach.competitor: Agents-as-teammates on project boards; Go backend + Next.js frontend
vs.multica.pr.agena: Auto branch + PR on GitHub and Azure DevOps
vs.multica.review.agena: Dedicated Reviewer agent with configurable model + prompt
vs.multica.dependencies.competitor: Board assignment, no explicit task dependency graph
vs.multica.sprint.competitor: Project boards; no Azure/Jira sprint import or SP estimation
vs.multica.why1: Sprint refinement: AGENA indexes your closed Azure/Jira items in Qdrant and grounds story-point estimates in your team's real history — not generic heuristics.
vs.multica.why2: Multi-repo orchestration: One task can fan out to N repos in parallel, each with its own branch, PR, and per-repo lock. Multica is single-repo per task.
vs.multica.why3: Visual flow builder: n8n-style pipeline editor with Agent, HTTP, Condition, Azure DevOps, GitHub, New Relic, and Notify nodes — no YAML or code needed.
vs.multica.why4: Production telemetry built in: Sentry + New Relic auto-import, DORA metrics dashboard, team-health symptom analysis — the feedback loop from prod error to AI fix to merged PR is end-to-end.
vs.multica.metaTitle: AGENA vs Multica – Agentic Coding Platform Comparison
vs.multica.metaDesc: Compare AGENA and Multica, two open-source agentic coding platforms. See how AGENA's multi-repo orchestration, Qdrant-grounded sprint refinement, and DORA analytics compare to Multica's agent-as-teammate dashboard.
sprintSwitcher.title: Active Sprint
sprintSwitcher.jiraNotConnected: Jira not connected. Connect it from Integrations.
refinement.deleteCommentBody: All comments prefixed with [{signature}] will be deleted from this work item on {provider}. This cannot be undone.
refinement.recommendedAuthor.empty.title: Recommended assignee
refinement.recommendedAuthor.empty.noRep: To get a code-author suggestion, open Advanced ▾ → Repo on the right and pick the repo this work lives in, then re-run refinement.
refinement.recommendedAuthor.empty.noMat: No matching team member has committed to the touched files in the last 6 months. Wrong repo? Or your team's emails differ between Azure/Jira and git authorship.
refinement.recommendedAuthor.empty.noFil: The LLM couldn't pin down concrete files to touch (description may be too vague). Enrich the description, double-check the repo, and try again.
refinement.runs.title: My refinement runs
refinement.runs.subtitle: Analysis and writeback records grouped by sprint · {total} total
dora.hub.subtitle: Each card below shows DORA metrics computed from that repo's synced git data. Click Sync on a repo to pull its latest commits, PRs, and deployments.
dora.hub.noReposBody: Add your repos at Integrations → Repo mappings, then come back to sync DORA data.
landing.skillsExample.label: How it works in practice
landing.skillsExample.pastBody: #61981 — Color variant link routes to wrong product. Senior dev fixes it by adding a fallback in ColorVariantResolver and tightening the slug regex.
landing.skillsExample.skillBody: name: Color-variant routing fallback · tags: routing, slug, e-commerce · approach: route through ColorVariantResolver, never inline the slug parse.
landing.skillsExample.futureBody: #64044 — Category filter loses variant on pagination. Top skill match (81%) is the routing fallback above. Its prompt fragment is injected: agent skips inline slug parse, reuses the resolver, ships a fix consistent with the team's precedent.
skills.importDefaultsConfirm: Add the curated default skills to this org? Existing entries with the same name will be skipped.
skills.importDefaultsResult: Imported: {inserted} new, {skipped} already existed, {total} total in the bundle.
integrations.sentry.aiFixImport: Import as task → Let AI fix it
integrations.sentry.heroSubtitle: AI-powered error triage. Import production errors, get root-cause analysis with one click, ship fixes through your repo.
integrations.newrelic.heroSubtitle: Auto-import APM errors from your New Relic entities, route them to the right repo, let AI fix and ship the patch.
integrations.datadog.heroSubtitle: Pull Error Tracking issues straight from Datadog, AI fixes them through your repo.
integrations.datadog.emptyHint: Adjust the query or pick a wider time range and hit Fetch.
integrations.appdynamics.heroSubtitle: Pull APM errors from AppDynamics into Agena, AI fixes them through your repo.
integrations.secretSavedHint: Secret already saved — leave blank to keep it.
integrationRules.title: Integration Rules
integrationRules.heroSubtitle: Auto-tag and auto-route imported tasks based on reporter, issue type, project, or labels.
integrationRules.emptyHint: Add a rule to auto-tag tasks coming in from this provider — for example: tasks reported by your security team get a "security" tag and auto-route to the security review flow.
integrationRules.labelsHint: Suggestions appear after Jira labels load.
integrationRules.tagsHintAzure: Suggestions appear after Azure tags load (requires a default project in /dashboard/sprints).
agents.role.securityDeveloper.label: Security Developer
agents.role.securityDeveloper.descriptio: Adversarial security reviewer. Treats every input as malicious, every dependency as compromised. OWASP Top 10 + race conditions, IDOR, SSRF, mass assignment. Ships fixes with threat-model, regression test, residual-risk notes.
agents.role.securityDeveloper.prompt: You are a Senior Security Engineer. Treat every input as malicious. Reproduce the issue, patch with the smallest blast radius, add a regression test, document the threat model, list residual risks.
agents.errRoleRequired: Role could not be derived — fill the name.
agents.errRoleDuplicate: An agent with role "{role}" already exists.
reviews.title: Agent Reviews
reviews.heroSubtitle: AI-driven adversarial code reviews. Track findings, severities, and per-agent verdict history without touching the code.
reviews.emptyHint: Hit the 🔎 Review button on any task to run a reviewer agent against it.
reviews.searchPlaceholder: Search task title, output, or task #…
reviews.noFilterHint: Clear filters or pick a different combo.
agents.toggleReviewerDesc: When on, this agent shows up in the 🔎 Review dropdowns on tasks and on the Reviews tab. Reviews never modify code or open PRs.
insights.title: Cross-Source Insights
insights.subtitle: Clusters of related events from PRs, deploys, monitoring, and tickets — auto-detected every 5 minutes. Confidence ≥ 70 surfaces here.
insights.empty.title: No correlations yet
insights.empty.body: AGENA is watching for clusters. As soon as a PR merge correlates with a Sentry / NewRelic / Jira event in the same window, you'll see it here. The poller runs every 5 minutes — or click Scan now.
triage.title: Stale Ticket Triage
triage.subtitle: AI evaluates tickets that have been idle past your threshold and suggests close / snooze / keep. Bulk-approve to apply.
triage.set.sourcesHint: Which task.source values to scan. Default: jira,azure_devops
triage.empty.title: Backlog is clean
triage.empty.body: No stale tickets to triage. AGENA will rescan periodically — or click Scan now.
backlog.title: Review Backlog
backlog.subtitle: PRs sitting unreviewed past your warn / critical thresholds. Nudge reviewers from here.
backlog.set.exemptHint: Comma-separated repo mapping ids to skip.
backlog.empty.title: No PRs in the backlog
backlog.empty.body: Every open PR is within the review window. AGENA polls every 30 minutes — or click Scan now.
